era: 1970s-present. Hard Rock. E=7 -- the voice. Seattle. Solo career documentation separate from Heart (234-235).
born: June 19, 1950, San Diego, California (raised Seattle, WA)
role_in_heart: Lead vocalist. The instrument around which all Heart arrangements are built. The center of gravity of the band.
distinctive_from_nancy_wilson: Ann = vocals, belt technique, emotional center. Nancy = guitar, composition, film scoring. Two individuals documented at two separate degrees (259, 260).

vocal_technique.range: Wide -- warm chest voice low register to soprano high register
vocal_technique.primary_technique: Full-body belt: chest voice resonance driven into upper registers with control
vocal_technique.vibrato: Natural, wide oscillation, moderate rate, appears spontaneously on held notes
vocal_technique.emotional_quality: Direct emotional inhabitation of material -- not distanced, fully committed
vocal_technique.comparison: Wider range than Pat Benatar; more power-focused than Grace Slick; more controlled than Janis Joplin

peak_performances.label: Portrait
peak_performances.notes: The defining vocal document. Full power from first word. Controlled high-register over aggressive guitar. Used in music schools as hard rock vocal technique exemplar.

peak_performances.label: Capitol
peak_performances.notes: Power ballad. Dynamic control: held back then full release. One of the most-covered ballads of its era.

solo_discography.title: Hope & Glory
solo_discography.notes: Cover album: Led Zeppelin, The Who, U2, Elton John. Voice inhabiting other artists' material.

solo_discography.title: Immortal
solo_discography.notes: Tribute covers: Bowie, Lennon, Gregg Allman. 'Life on Mars' cited as highlight.

theory_nodes.concept: Ann Wilson created the female hard rock vocal template. In 1975-1977 there was no commercial precedent for a female vocalist with hard rock range and belt power. She created the category. Lineage forward: Lzzy Hale (Halestorm), Amy Lee (Evanescence), Taylor Momsen (The Pretty Reckless) -- all cite Wilson directly.
theory_nodes.description: The female hard rock vocal tradition originates with Ann Wilson.

theory_nodes.concept: The belt technique. Full-body belt = chest voice resonance driven up into higher registers beyond comfortable chest range. Produces controlled power, not screaming. The technical distinction between Wilson's approach and untrained shouting is this control. "Barracuda" is the definitive demonstration.
theory_nodes.description: Belt as controlled power: Ann Wilson's primary vocal contribution.

theory_nodes.concept: Wilson sisters separated at degrees 259-260. Heart (234-235) documents the band as a unit. Ann (259) and Nancy (260) receive individual documentation of distinct careers and contributions. Ann: vocals, emotional center, belt, 'Barracuda', 'Alone'. Nancy: guitar, film scoring, 'Almost Famous'. Back-to-back degrees form a complete sisters portrait.

cross_references.relationship: Band entry. Ann and Nancy together. Full Heart arc from Mushroom Records to Capitol Records.
Second Heart entry. 1980s hard rock mode. 'Alone' era.
The guitar. Film scoring. Distinct career, distinct instrument. Back-to-back with Ann forms complete Wilson sisters portrait.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=7 vs E=8. Hard rock belt vs. rolling crack and rumble. Both: large natural forces producing sustained sound from physical energy conversion. Vocal belt = biological; thunder = atmospheric. Both organize energy into sound. Storm louder; voice more precise.

storm_arc_position.note: FINAL E=8 in SPS streak. Degree 260 brings E=9 NEW ABSOLUTE SPS MAXIMUM.
